title: Functions - Functions on objects - Objects and links - Palantir

extractedEntities.name: Function
extractedEntities.description: TypeScript-based logic that interacts with Ontology objects and links, enabling programmatic access to properties, link traversals, and search-around operations

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: A core Ontology entity whose properties are converted to fields on a generated TypeScript interface, accessible via dot notation in Functions

extractedEntities.name: Property
extractedEntities.description: A characteristic of an object type, converted to a TypeScript field that may return undefined if no concrete value is set

extractedEntities.name: Link Type
extractedEntities.description: A relationship between object types converted to fields on TypeScript interfaces, supporting 1-to-1, 1-to-many, and many-to-many cardinalities

extractedEntities.name: ObjectSet
extractedEntities.description: A lazy collection of objects that can be traversed via links without loading individual object instances into memory

extractedEntities.name: SingleLink
extractedEntities.description: Generated TypeScript type for the '1' side of a link, providing get() and getAsync() methods to retrieve the linked object

extractedEntities.name: MultiLink
extractedEntities.description: Generated TypeScript type for the 'many' side of a link, providing all() and allAsync() methods to retrieve an array of linked objects

extractedEntities.name: Search Around
extractedEntities.description: APIs generated on ObjectSets when links are created, enabling navigation from one object set to linked object sets without loading individual objects

extractedEntities.name: ReadOnlyArray
extractedEntities.description: Immutable array type used for array properties and link traversal results, requiring explicit copy to modify

extractedEntities.name: Lazy Link Traversal
extractedEntities.description: Pattern of traversing links as ObjectSets to avoid loading linked object instances into memory, improving performance for large datasets

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's core platform that hosts the Ontology and generates TypeScript APIs for object types and their links

keyInsights: Object type properties are code-generated into TypeScript interfaces with dot-notation access, and may return undefined requiring explicit null handling
Array properties use ReadOnlyArray to enforce immutability semantics -- modifications require creating a copy first
Link traversal supports both eager loading (get/all methods) and lazy ObjectSet-based traversal via Search Around APIs to avoid memory overhead
Search Around APIs are automatically generated when links are created in the Ontology, enabling ObjectSet-to-ObjectSet navigation
Link types support three cardinalities: 1-to-1, 1-to-many, and many-to-many, each with different generated TypeScript types (SingleLink vs MultiLink)

title: Models in the Ontology - Palantir

extractedEntities.name: Model
extractedEntities.description: A machine learning or AI model integrated into the Ontology, deployed for live inference and mapped to Ontology concepts for interpretability and reuse

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Core Ontology entity whose properties can serve as inputs and outputs for ML models, enabling interpretable modeling results

extractedEntities.name: ObjectSet
extractedEntities.description: A collection of objects that can be passed as input to ML models for batch or live inference

extractedEntities.name: Property
extractedEntities.description: Attributes of object types used by models as input features or output targets, making ML results interpretable as real-world concepts like forecasts or classifications

extractedEntities.name: Function
extractedEntities.description: Wrapper logic published around a deployed model to orchestrate complex inference and expose it to end-user applications

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Platform providing the data foundation, model evaluation tools, and deployment capabilities for operationalizing AI/ML

extractedEntities.name: Workshop
extractedEntities.description: End-user application where deployed model functions can be consumed for live inference

extractedEntities.name: Vertex
extractedEntities.description: End-user application for consuming deployed model functions alongside Workshop

extractedEntities.name: Digital Twin
extractedEntities.description: Pattern where the Ontology becomes a single source of truth for both data and logic, enabling enterprise-wide simulation of future changes using ML models

extractedEntities.name: Direct Model Deployment
extractedEntities.description: Configuration that deploys a model for live inference, making it callable via wrapper functions in the Ontology

extractedEntities.name: Batch Inference
extractedEntities.description: Using a model in Code Repositories to produce datasets that back Ontology objects, as opposed to live inference

keyInsights: Models are integrated into the Ontology by mapping their inputs/outputs to object type properties, making ML results interpretable as real-world concepts like forecasts, estimates, or classifications
The end-to-end ML operationalization workflow is: create model, configure deployment, publish wrapper function, consume in user-facing applications
Modeling efforts achieve economies of scale because forecasts and predictions produced for one use case can immediately serve subsequent use cases through the shared Ontology
The Ontology becomes a digital twin encoding both data and organizational logic/expectations, enabling enterprise-wide simulation and future-state prediction
Models can operate in two modes: live inference (via direct model deployment + functions) and batch inference (via Code Repositories backing object type datasets)

title: Derived properties - Palantir

extractedEntities.name: Derived Property
extractedEntities.description: A property calculated at runtime based on other properties or links, including aggregations on linked objects, available for filtering, sorting, and aggregation within the same request

extractedEntities.name: Property
extractedEntities.description: A stored characteristic of an object type that can serve as the basis for derived property calculations

extractedEntities.name: Link Type
extractedEntities.description: Relationship between object types used by derived properties to access and aggregate values from linked objects

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Core Ontology entity on which derived properties are defined, with support currently limited to OSv2-indexed types

extractedEntities.name: withProperties Operation
extractedEntities.description: TypeScript OSDK operation for requesting derived properties, available in @osdk/client 2.2.0-beta.x or later

extractedEntities.name: Linked Property Aggregation
extractedEntities.description: Type of derived property that aggregates or selects values from 1:1 or 1:N linked objects at runtime

extractedEntities.name: Column Math
extractedEntities.description: Type of derived property that combines multiple properties of the same object using mathematical expressions

extractedEntities.name: OSDK
extractedEntities.description: Ontology SDK providing TypeScript client for accessing derived properties via the withProperties operation

extractedEntities.name: Derived Property Security
extractedEntities.description: Security model where derived properties use the security of all objects involved in the calculation, never exposing information a user could not otherwise see

keyInsights: Derived properties are computed at runtime (not stored) and can be based on other properties, link traversals, or aggregations on linked objects
Two main types of derived properties exist: linked property/aggregation (from 1:1 or 1:N links) and column math (combining multiple properties on the same object)
Derived properties inherit the security model of all objects involved in their calculation, ensuring no information leakage
Currently a beta feature with limitations: no OSv1 support, no text search, no struct properties in OSDK queries (workaround: use $select to exclude structs)
Derived properties can chain into further operations -- once computed they can be used for additional filtering, sorting, or aggregation within the same request

title: Object and link types - Metadata - Statuses - Palantir

extractedEntities.name: Status
extractedEntities.description: Metadata indicating the developmental state of an Ontology resource: active, experimental, deprecated, example, or endorsed (object types only)

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Core Ontology entity that supports all status values including the special endorsed status, with cascading status changes to child properties

extractedEntities.name: Property
extractedEntities.description: Characteristic of an object type whose status can be automatically cascaded when the parent object type's status changes

extractedEntities.name: Link Type
extractedEntities.description: Relationship between object types that carries its own developmental status metadata

extractedEntities.name: Action Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology resource for defining data modification operations that carries developmental status metadata

extractedEntities.name: Interface
extractedEntities.description: Ontology abstraction mechanism that carries developmental status metadata

extractedEntities.name: Resource Lifecycle
extractedEntities.description: Pattern for managing Ontology resources through developmental stages: experimental to active to deprecated, with endorsed as a higher trust level

extractedEntities.name: Ontology Owner Role
extractedEntities.description: Role required to directly apply the endorsed status to an object type; other users must submit proposals for owner approval

extractedEntities.name: Endorsed Status
extractedEntities.description: Beta status for object types only, signifying a core trusted resource vetted by ontology owners, with automatic visibility set to prominent and protections on API name changes

extractedEntities.name: Deprecated Status Metadata
extractedEntities.description: Additional metadata on deprecated resources including deprecation reason, expected deletion deadline, and replacement resource reference

keyInsights: Every Ontology resource (object types, properties, link types, actions, interfaces) has a developmental status that communicates its readiness for production use

Five status values exist: active (in production), experimental (under development), deprecated (slated for deletion), example (notional/training), and endorsed (core trusted resource, object types only)
Status changes cascade: changing an object type from active to experimental automatically marks all its properties as experimental
Endorsed status is restricted to object types and requires the Ontology Owner role to apply directly; other users must submit proposals
Deprecated resources carry rich metadata: deprecation reason, expected deletion deadline, and a reference to the replacement resource
Endorsed status automatically sets visibility to prominent, increasing discoverability, and inherits the same operational protections as active status (e.g., deletion restrictions)

title: Object and link types - Metadata - Render hints - Palantir

extractedEntities.name: Render Hint
extractedEntities.description: Metadata on Ontology properties that communicates usage information to Object Storage and user applications, controlling indexing, display, and interaction behavior

extractedEntities.name: Property
extractedEntities.description: Characteristic of an object type to which render hints are applied, controlling how the property is indexed and displayed

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Core Ontology entity whose properties carry render hints, with some hints requiring a full reindex to take effect

extractedEntities.name: Sortable Render Hint
extractedEntities.description: Enables sorting on a property in applications like timelines and charts; adds a raw index and requires reindex

extractedEntities.name: Searchable Render Hint
extractedEntities.description: Enables filtering and aggregation on a property; prerequisite for low-cardinality filtering; adds raw index and requires reindex

extractedEntities.name: Selectable Render Hint
extractedEntities.description: Enables aggregation on string properties; adds raw index and requires reindex

extractedEntities.name: Visibility Render Hint
extractedEntities.description: Controls display prominence of a property in applications: prominent (shown first), normal, or hidden

extractedEntities.name: Low Cardinality Render Hint
extractedEntities.description: Indicates few possible values for a property, enabling specific filter widgets; requires the Searchable hint and adds raw index

extractedEntities.name: Object Storage V1 (Phonograph)
extractedEntities.description: Legacy object storage system that uses render hints to optimize indexing behavior and column storage

extractedEntities.name: Object Explorer
extractedEntities.description: User application that uses render hints to determine property display, filtering, and sorting capabilities

keyInsights: Render hints are a metadata system that bridges Ontology property definitions and their storage/display behavior in applications and Object Storage
Some render hints add raw indexes in Object Storage V1, causing two columns to be counted per property -- deselecting them improves reindex performance
Render hints fall into two categories: those applied immediately when saved, and those requiring a full reindex of the backing datasource to take effect
The Searchable hint is a prerequisite for Low Cardinality, creating a dependency chain between render hints
Visibility render hint controls prominence in applications: prominent properties appear first, hidden properties are not shown at all, normal is the default
Render hints like Identifier optimize reindex performance by indicating that numerical keys do not need number formatting or range filtering

title: Object and link types - Object types - Create an object type - Palantir

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Core Ontology entity created via a guided helper in Ontology Manager, requiring metadata, backing datasource, property mappings, and key configuration

extractedEntities.name: Primary Key
extractedEntities.description: Unique identifier property required for every object type, ensuring each object instance is uniquely identifiable

extractedEntities.name: Title Key
extractedEntities.description: Required property designating the human-readable display name for objects of a type, shown in user applications

extractedEntities.name: Backing Datasource
extractedEntities.description: A Foundry dataset or external source that provides the underlying data for an object type; a single datasource can only back one object type

extractedEntities.name: Property
extractedEntities.description: Attribute of an object type, automatically mapped from backing datasource columns or manually configured

extractedEntities.name: API Name
extractedEntities.description: Programmatic identifier for an object type used in code, assignable after creation and distinct from the display name

extractedEntities.name: Object Type Groups
extractedEntities.description: Categorization mechanism for organizing object types within the Ontology, making it easier to filter and find relevant types

extractedEntities.name: Ontology Manager
extractedEntities.description: Foundry application providing the guided helper interface for creating, configuring, and managing object types and their metadata

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Platform hosting datasets that serve as backing datasources for object types

extractedEntities.name: Guided Object Creation
extractedEntities.description: Step-by-step wizard pattern for creating object types: choose datasource, set metadata, create properties, configure keys, generate actions, save

keyInsights: Every object type requires at minimum a primary key (unique identifier) and a title key (human-readable display name)
A single Foundry datasource can only back one object type, enforcing a 1:1 mapping between backing data and object type definitions
Backing datasources cannot contain MapType or StructType columns, constraining the data shapes that can directly back object types
When a backing datasource is selected, its columns are automatically mapped to properties, but any can be discarded during the configuration step
Object types can be created without an existing datasource -- an empty dataset is generated at a chosen location for permissions management
The guided creation workflow follows a fixed sequence: datasource selection, metadata, properties, key configuration, action generation, save location, and commit

title: Object and link types - Object types - Metadata reference - Palantir

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Core Ontology entity represented by comprehensive metadata including ID, RID, icon, display names, description, groups, API name, visibility, status, index status, and writeback configuration

extractedEntities.name: Object Type ID
extractedEntities.description: Unique human-readable identifier used to reference objects of a type in application configuration, e.g., 'employee'

extractedEntities.name: Object Type RID
extractedEntities.description: Automatically generated unique resource identifier for every Foundry resource, referenced in error messages across the platform

extractedEntities.name: API Name
extractedEntities.description: Programmatic name used when referring to the object type in code, distinct from the display name

extractedEntities.name: Visibility
extractedEntities.description: Metadata controlling how prominently an object type is displayed in applications: prominent (shown first), normal (default), or hidden (not shown)

extractedEntities.name: Index Status
extractedEntities.description: Status of the last reindex of the object type and its backing datasources: success, failed, or not started

extractedEntities.name: Writeback Configuration
extractedEntities.description: Metadata indicating whether the object type has a writeback dataset and whether end-user edits are enabled or disabled

extractedEntities.name: Status
extractedEntities.description: Developmental state metadata: active, experimental, or deprecated, with experimental as the default for new object types

extractedEntities.name: Property
extractedEntities.description: Characteristics of an object type that form part of its full metadata specification

keyInsights: Object types have a dual identity system: a human-readable ID (e.g., 'employee') for configuration and an auto-generated RID for platform-level error messages and references
Visibility is a three-level system (prominent, normal, hidden) that controls discoverability in user applications, separate from the developmental status
New object types default to experimental status, requiring explicit promotion to active for production use
Writeback configuration is a distinct metadata field that controls whether end users can edit objects of a type, independent of the object type's datasource
API name is the programmatic identifier distinct from the display name, enabling code-stable references even when display names change

title: Object edits and materializations - How user edits are applied - Palantir

extractedEntities.name: Action Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology mechanism for applying data modifications to objects, links, or object sets, triggering immediate index updates and periodic persistent storage flushes

extractedEntities.name: Writeback
extractedEntities.description: Mechanism for persisting user edits from object databases into Foundry datasets, enabling the most up-to-date object state to be read

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Core Ontology entity whose instances can be edited through Actions, with version control ensuring transactional consistency

extractedEntities.name: Property
extractedEntities.description: Attributes of objects that can be modified through Action-based edits, subject to version control

extractedEntities.name: Funnel Service
extractedEntities.description: Service that receives modification instructions from Actions, stores them in an offset-tracked queue supporting simultaneous edits

extractedEntities.name: Object Storage V2
extractedEntities.description: Object storage system that tracks Funnel offsets for object types and many-to-many link types, applying edits to live indexed data with read-after-write consistency

extractedEntities.name: Schema Migration Framework
extractedEntities.description: Object Storage V2 capability for migrating user edits, including a 'drop all edits' instruction to reset objects to their input datasource state

extractedEntities.name: Entity Version Control
extractedEntities.description: Mechanism ensuring transactional consistency when applying Actions by checking version consistency of both object type definitions and object instances

extractedEntities.name: Optimistic Concurrency
extractedEntities.description: Pattern where front-end consumers pass object version on Action submission, and the server applies the Action only if versions match, using compare-and-swap

extractedEntities.name: Object Storage V1 (Phonograph)
extractedEntities.description: Legacy object storage without schema migration support, where removing writeback dataset configuration deletes all existing user edits

extractedEntities.name: Materialization
extractedEntities.description: Process of periodically flushing in-memory edits from the object database index to persistent Foundry datasets via the Funnel service

keyInsights: When an Action is applied, edits are immediately applied to the object database index and periodically flushed to persistent Foundry datasets via the Funnel service
Object Storage V2 guarantees read-after-write consistency: any object read occurring after a user modification will contain the user edits
There is no mechanism to undo a single user edit -- corrections require additional user edits or object recreation
Object Storage V2 provides a schema migration framework with a 'drop all edits' instruction for resetting to input datasource state, useful for transitioning from testing to production
Version control uses optimistic concurrency: the front-end passes the object version seen by the user, and the server uses compare-and-swap to ensure the Action applies to the correct version
Object Storage V1 (Phonograph) and V2 have different version control behaviors, with V2 providing more robust transactional guarantees

title: Ontologies - Overview - Palantir

extractedEntities.name: Ontology
extractedEntities.description: Top-level artifact that stores ontological resources (object types, link types, action types, interfaces, shared properties, groups), mapped 1:1 with a space

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Core Ontology resource representing a real-world entity, contained within an ontology

extractedEntities.name: Link Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology resource defining relationships between object types, contained within an ontology

extractedEntities.name: Action Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology resource defining data modification operations, contained within an ontology

extractedEntities.name: Interface
extractedEntities.description: Ontology resource for abstraction and polymorphism, contained within an ontology

extractedEntities.name: Shared Property
extractedEntities.description: Ontology resource enabling property definitions to be reused across multiple object types within an ontology

extractedEntities.name: Object Type Group
extractedEntities.description: Organizational unit for categorizing object types within an ontology

extractedEntities.name: Space
extractedEntities.description: Foundry organizational unit mapped 1:1 with an ontology; when a space is created, a corresponding ontology is simultaneously created with the same organization markings

extractedEntities.name: Organization Markings
extractedEntities.description: Security mechanism controlling which organizations can access ontological entities; private ontologies belong to one organization, shared ontologies span multiple

extractedEntities.name: Shared Ontology
extractedEntities.description: Pattern where an ontology is shared among multiple organizations, enabling safe cross-organization data and workflow sharing

keyInsights: An ontology is the top-level container for all Ontology resources: object types, link types, action types, interfaces, shared properties, and object type groups
Ontologies are mapped 1:1 with spaces -- creating a new space automatically creates a corresponding ontology with the same organization markings
Ontologies can be either private (single organization) or shared (multiple organizations), enabling controlled cross-organization collaboration
Shared ontologies inherit the markings of shared spaces, ensuring that only users of specified organizations can access ontological entities
Shared properties are an explicit Ontology resource type, enabling property definitions to be reused across multiple object types

title: Foundry Branching - Overview - Palantir

extractedEntities.name: Foundry Branching
extractedEntities.description: Platform capability for developing and testing end-to-end workflows (code, data pipelines, Ontology, Workshop) in isolation on a branch without disrupting production

extractedEntities.name: Pipeline Builder
extractedEntities.description: Foundry application for building data pipelines, supported by Foundry Branching for isolated development

extractedEntities.name: Workshop
extractedEntities.description: User-facing application builder supported by Foundry Branching, with some non-Workshop elements (e.g., Quiver dashboards) not modifiable on branches

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Core platform providing the branching infrastructure and merge capabilities across multiple application types

extractedEntities.name: Ontology Proposal
extractedEntities.description: Mechanism within Foundry Branching for proposing, reviewing, and merging Ontology changes from a development branch to production

extractedEntities.name: Branch Isolation
extractedEntities.description: Pattern of making changes to transforms, pipelines, Ontology, and applications on a single branch, testing end-to-end, then merging with a single click

extractedEntities.name: Release Management
extractedEntities.description: Complementary pattern to branching for managing multiple versions of resources across distinct environments (dev, staging, production)

extractedEntities.name: Action Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology resource whose execution can be run on branches without writing back edits to Main

extractedEntities.name: Branch Retention Policy
extractedEntities.description: Configuration for automatically closing stagnant branches, as branches are intended to be short-lived to avoid conflicts and reduce costs

keyInsights: Foundry Branching provides a unified experience for making changes across code repositories, Pipeline Builder, the Ontology, and Workshop on a single branch
Actions can be run on branches without writing back edits to Main, enabling safe testing of data modification workflows
Branches are intended to be short-lived and small to avoid conflicts, with retention policies to automatically close stagnant branches
Modifying large object types on a branch can incur significant additional compute and storage costs
Foundry Branching and release management are complementary: branching for development/testing isolation, release management for managing versions across environments
A review process with per-resource approval policies ensures changes are finalized and approved before merging into the Main branch

title: Why create an Ontology? - Palantir

extractedEntities.name: Ontology
extractedEntities.description: Shared source of truth for decision-making and decision capture across an organization, abstracting away technical concepts to present data in standard business terms

extractedEntities.name: Connectivity at Scale
extractedEntities.description: Pattern where the Ontology enables users to discover data across the organization and view local decisions in a global context, providing a single source of truth

extractedEntities.name: Interpretability
extractedEntities.description: Pattern where the Ontology abstracts away technical concepts (datasets, joins, code) allowing non-technical decision makers to engage with data in standard terms

extractedEntities.name: Economies of Scale
extractedEntities.description: Pattern where new information modeled into the Ontology becomes immediately available to all use cases, reducing duplicated effort and reinvention

extractedEntities.name: Decision Capture
extractedEntities.description: Pattern where the Ontology is used not only to read data but also to write data back and capture decisions made by users

extractedEntities.name: Energy Well Management
extractedEntities.description: An energy company using the Ontology to create a shared view of well health across petroleum engineers, integrity engineers, and management staff

extractedEntities.name: Aircraft Sensor Monitoring
extractedEntities.description: Manufacturing customer using the Ontology to make aircraft sensor monitoring data accessible to designers without requiring knowledge of tables or joins

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Platform providing Ontology-aware applications for analysis, workflow development, and decision capture

keyInsights: The Ontology provides five key organizational benefits: connectivity at scale, interpretability, economies of scale, decision capture, and powering operational AI/ML
The Ontology goes beyond a data catalog by defining a shared language that abstracts away technical concepts like datasets and joins for non-technical users
The Ontology is bidirectional: used not only to read data but to write back and capture decisions, making it an active decision-making system
Operating from standard data lakes leads to unmanageable complexity over time, while the Ontology provides a well-defined system that scales with data growth
The Ontology enables cross-functional collaboration by providing a common language, eliminating lengthy reconciliation processes to confirm everyone sees the same data

title: Understanding Palantir's Ontology: Semantic, Kinetic, and Dynamic Layers Explained

extractedEntities.name: Semantic Layer
extractedEntities.description: Core ontology layer defining the conceptual model: what entities exist, how they relate, and what properties they have -- answering 'what are the things that matter'

extractedEntities.name: Kinetic Layer
extractedEntities.description: Ontology layer connecting the conceptual model to actual data sources via ETL pipelines, enabling traceable and auditable data lineage from source to insight

extractedEntities.name: Dynamic Layer
extractedEntities.description: Ontology layer introducing behavior through business rules, access control, lifecycle management, and workflow enforcement

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Entities defined in the semantic layer representing real-world concepts like Person, Vehicle, Transaction, Organization

extractedEntities.name: Property
extractedEntities.description: Attributes defined in the semantic layer such as name, timestamp, representing characteristics of entities

extractedEntities.name: Link Type
extractedEntities.description: Relationships defined in the semantic layer connecting entities, e.g., Person owns Vehicle

extractedEntities.name: Action Type
extractedEntities.description: Part of the kinetic layer operationalizing the ontology model with data feeds and executable functions

extractedEntities.name: Function
extractedEntities.description: Part of the kinetic layer enabling complex logic and data orchestration around the ontology model

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Platform where engineers build the kinetic layer and end users interact through the dynamic layer

extractedEntities.name: Gotham
extractedEntities.description: Palantir platform that also uses the ontology concept with semantic, kinetic, and dynamic layers

extractedEntities.name: Three-Layer Ontology Architecture
extractedEntities.description: Architectural pattern organizing the ontology into semantic (conceptual model), kinetic (data connection), and dynamic (behavior/rules) layers

keyInsights: The Palantir Ontology is organized into three distinct layers: semantic (what exists), kinetic (connecting to real data), and dynamic (business rules and behavior)
The semantic layer defines entities, relationships, and properties as a shared language that reconciles fragmented data concepts across sources
The kinetic layer maps raw data (SQL tables, CSVs, APIs) to ontological entities via ETL pipelines with traceable data lineage
The dynamic layer introduces active behavior: business rules, access control, and lifecycle management (e.g., Suspect to Investigated to Cleared)
Different roles interact with different layers: analysts use semantic, engineers build kinetic, end users interact through dynamic
The layered approach enables the ontology to be not just a passive model but an active, adaptive system

title: Palantir Foundry: Ontology - Jimmy Wang - Medium

extractedEntities.name: Ontology
extractedEntities.description: Structured semantic layer in Foundry that organizes and connects data, providing a consistent framework of objects, attributes, and relationships

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Entity definition in the Ontology representing real-world concepts like Customer, Order, or Machine, with typed attributes and relationships

extractedEntities.name: Property
extractedEntities.description: Attributes assigned to object types such as numeric, text, or categorical data (e.g., Customer Name, Order Date)

extractedEntities.name: Link Type
extractedEntities.description: Relationships between object types representing interactions or dependencies, supporting One-to-Many and Many-to-Many cardinalities

extractedEntities.name: Action Type
extractedEntities.description: Part of the Action Framework for performing operations on objects such as updating order status or assigning tasks

extractedEntities.name: Ontology Manager
extractedEntities.description: Foundry tool for creating object types, adding attributes, and setting relationships

extractedEntities.name: Workshop
extractedEntities.description: Application platform for filtering and visualizing objects based on their Ontology attributes and relationships

extractedEntities.name: Order-Customer-Product Model
extractedEntities.description: Example ontology with Order, Customer, and Product object types linked via One-to-Many and Many-to-Many relationships through Order Line Items

extractedEntities.name: Ontology Construction Workflow
extractedEntities.description: Four-step pattern: define object types, add attributes, set relationships, map data from raw datasets through pipelines

keyInsights: The Ontology construction follows a four-step process: define object types, add attributes (properties), set relationships (links), and map raw data through pipelines
Link types support multiple cardinalities: Order to Customer is One-to-Many, Order to Product is Many-to-Many via Order Line Items as a join table
Once built, the Ontology feeds three major downstream applications: Workshop (visualization), Action Framework (operations), and AI Models (ML workflows)
Raw datasets from diverse sources (databases, logs, APIs, CSVs) are mapped to Ontology object types through data pipelines, creating a unified knowledge system
The Ontology serves as a shared language for datasets, pipelines, and applications, enabling consistent downstream analysis

title: Foundational Ontologies in Palantir Foundry - Dorian Smiley - Medium

extractedEntities.name: Foundational Ontology
extractedEntities.description: Design pattern for creating base ontology structures that can be extended for specific use cases, serving as reusable foundations for enterprise knowledge modeling

extractedEntities.name: Ontology
extractedEntities.description: Structured, machine-processable format encapsulating human knowledge about domain relationships, functioning as a form of artificial intelligence through knowledge graphs

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Core entity in the ontology representing real-world concepts, defined through data modeling processes that uncover hidden relationships

extractedEntities.name: Property
extractedEntities.description: Attributes of ontological entities encoding characteristics of real-world objects

extractedEntities.name: Link Type
extractedEntities.description: Semantic relationships between entities that mirror the complex web of connections within real-world datasets

extractedEntities.name: Shared Property
extractedEntities.description: Reusable property definitions that can be shared across multiple object types in a foundational ontology

extractedEntities.name: Knowledge Graph
extractedEntities.description: Graph-based data model underlying the ontology that enables AI systems to make inferences, recognize patterns, and predict outcomes

extractedEntities.name: Ontology Extensibility
extractedEntities.description: Design principle where foundational ontologies provide base patterns that can be extended and specialized for specific use cases

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir platform where foundational ontologies are built and extended for enterprise use cases

extractedEntities.name: Predictive System State
extractedEntities.description: Using foundational ontologies to predict future system states based on encoded relationships between entities

keyInsights: Ontologies are themselves a form of artificial intelligence, encapsulating human knowledge into structured, machine-processable formats that enable inference and pattern recognition
Foundational ontologies serve as reusable base patterns that can be extended for specific use cases, preventing each modeling project from starting from scratch
The ontology design process involves: problem definition, data collection, preparation/cleaning, analysis (clustering, association rules, decision trees), and interpretation of results
Knowledge graphs underlying ontologies bridge the gap between raw data and meaningful insights by encoding both entities and semantic relationships
Ontologies enable AI agents to understand context, preferences, and nuances by providing a scaffold of human knowledge that aligns with human reasoning
The foundational ontology pattern reduces bias in logical models by continuously validating them against data-derived relationships

title: Palantir's Ontology, Kimball's Star Schema, and Model-Driven Data Engineering: A Comparative View

extractedEntities.name: Ontology
extractedEntities.description: Highly normalized, business-oriented abstraction layer in Foundry that models data as business objects with relationships as first-class citizens

extractedEntities.name: Business Object Modeling
extractedEntities.description: Pattern where data is modeled around real-world business entities (Customer, Account, Contract, Product, Event) rather than dimensional facts and measures

extractedEntities.name: Star Schema
extractedEntities.description: Kimball's dimensional modeling approach organizing data into denormalized facts and dimensions optimized for reporting, contrasted with the Ontology's normalized approach

extractedEntities.name: Normalization
extractedEntities.description: Data modeling principle where the Ontology uses highly normalized structures compared to the denormalized star schema approach

extractedEntities.name: Model-Driven Data Engineering
extractedEntities.description: Alternative approach combining business semantics and history preservation (like ontology) with pragmatic usability and performance (like dimensional modeling)

extractedEntities.name: Bi-temporal SCD2
extractedEntities.description: Slowly Changing Dimension Type 2 with bitemporal tracking, used in model-driven approaches for historization that bridges ontology and dimensional modeling

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Business objects in the Ontology such as Customer, Account, Contract, Product, Event that serve as the central modeling unit

extractedEntities.name: Link Type
extractedEntities.description: Relationships between business objects treated as first-class citizens in the Ontology, unlike foreign keys in traditional data modeling

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Platform providing the Ontology as its central semantic layer for business-oriented data modeling

keyInsights: Palantir's Ontology is a highly normalized approach compared to Kimball's denormalized star schema, trading reporting performance for semantic richness
In the Ontology, relationships between business objects are first-class citizens rather than foreign keys, enabling richer graph-like data navigation
The Ontology centers on business objects (Customer, Account, Contract) while star schema centers on facts and dimensions, representing fundamentally different modeling philosophies
A model-driven data engineering approach can bridge the gap by combining the ontology's business semantics with dimensional modeling's pragmatic performance
The Ontology's normalized structure aligns data around business concepts rather than query patterns, making it more adaptable to changing analytical needs
